title: Folklore the Affliction: Fall of the Spire - Part 4: Continuing the Adventure Recap
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=WD7EzA2sbnI

Chapter Two recap, with a brief detour to admit that *Hades* from Supergiant has eaten most of Daniel (@dungeondive)'s play time this week. He still gets a chapter done. The party descends into the city sewers, traces the Anatomist's escape route through a dungeon-tile maze, and walks into a flesh-construct ambush the killer left as a trap. The chapter ends with the heroes climbing a ladder back to street level — a *ladder* implemented as a new printed-meaning token, of course.

Daniel digs into a second expansion mechanic that's quietly impressing him: **charged abilities** on the new foes. Every successful enemy hit banks a charge token on its card; on the third charge, the foe triggers an unblockable signature move. It adds a soft timer to fights without imposing a hard one. Pairs beautifully with Folklore's existing *one-roll combat* — the percentile dice, the power die, and the target die all roll *together*, so a single shake resolves to-hit, damage type, and victim. The flesh construct's *front kick* outcome (target pushed for 2d6) is the showcase example.

Both heroes cross 120 lore for their first level-up choices. A watchman NPC companion was loaned to the party for the chapter; he gets bone-fractured and spooked before the curtain falls.

----

Folklore's *charged-abilities* mechanic puts a soft timer on every fight — three successful enemy hits and something nasty triggers. Do you prefer soft timers like this or hard turn-limit timers as a way to pace a combat?
